mappings.rationale: Directly covers the founding, development, and political and social life of the 13 English colonies in North America.
Spanish discovery, exploration, and conquest of the Caribbean, Mexico, Central America, and Peru from 1492 to c. 1550 are central to this section.
The founding of the 13 colonies, colonial economic and political development, and conflicts with the French all feed into the broader narrative of American history.
The section traces colonial self-government, loose royal control, and growing dissatisfaction that set the stage for the American Revolution.
The political culture of colonial America, including self-government, local political activity, and the American Philosophical Society, formed the Founders' milieu.
Spanish, Portuguese, English, French, and Dutch colonial empires in the Americas exemplify the varieties of European imperial expansion.
British mercantilism, the Navigation Acts, and the development of the English colonies in North America are significant chapters of British imperial history.
The introduction of slavery into the colonies, the African slave trade, and Indian slavery under the encomienda system are explicitly discussed.
The literary and cultural production of Spain's colonial empire and Portuguese Brazil form the corpus of colonial Latin American literature.
Indian policy, including slavery and peonage under the encomienda, the missionary role of the Church, and conflicts with the French and Indians, are covered.
Norse voyages to Greenland, early English exploration, and French exploration of North America are explicitly discussed.
Spanish conquest of Mexico and the destruction of the Aztec state provide the context for the establishment of Spain's colonial empire in the Americas.
Spanish colonization of the Yucatan Peninsula and Central America involved the subjugation of Maya peoples and their civilizations.
Mercantilism, the Navigation Acts, colonial economic exploitation, and the growth of agricultural and mining economies reflect early capitalist development.
The missionary role of the Roman Catholic Church in Spanish and Portuguese colonies and the Great Awakening in the English colonies are discussed.
The Roman Catholic Church's missionary role in Spanish and Portuguese colonies, including Indian policy and colonial administration, is explicitly covered.
Puritan settlements in New England were among the most significant English colonies, established for religious reasons discussed in the section.
The exploration and colonization of Brazil by the Portuguese, including colonial economic policies, slavery, and administration, are covered.
The colonial structures of European empires in the Americas -- encomienda, slavery, mercantilism -- are subjects of postcolonial critique.
The introduction of black slavery in both Spanish and English colonies across the Americas provides the historical context for abolitionist movements.
The colonial racial hierarchy, including Indian slavery, the introduction of black slaves, and the Brazilian racial mixture, relates to the history of racism.
Immigration to the colonies, social mobility, and the forced migration of enslaved peoples represent significant migratory movements.
The forced diaspora of enslaved Africans to the Americas and the voluntary emigration of Europeans constitute major diasporic movements.
The voyages of discovery and colonial trade routes across the Atlantic depended on advances in navigation discussed in this section.
Spanish colonial administration in Mexico, including the viceregal system and Indian policy, provides essential background for understanding later Mexican revolution.
The religious reasons for colonial settlement, the Great Awakening, and the beginning of religious diversity in the colonies are discussed.
Immigration and the rise of economic classes in the colonies, including the diverse origins of colonial settlers, are explicitly covered.
Colonial mercantilism, gold and silver mining, agricultural development, and smuggling and piracy connect to global economic patterns.
French and English colonial rivalries, the expulsion of French power from North America in 1763, and Spanish-Portuguese territorial disputes involve diplomatic history.
Colonial cultural and scientific achievements, including the American Philosophical Society, newspapers, public education, and the Great Awakening, are discussed.
